summary: Founding engineer turned Director of Engineering at Meridian Logistics, where I grew the team from just myself to 20+ engineers spanning backend, frontend, QA, DevOps, and release management, and delivered the infrastructure behind Meridian Logistics becoming the first regulated fintech platform in the US. I architected the backend systems and processes the platform is built on, took releases from ad hoc to a structured bi-weekly cadence across 4 engineering teams, and led the engineering and compliance work across a 7-year regulatory build-out. Hands-on with Node.js and TypeScript, and equally comfortable shaping technical strategy with C-suite leadership, I'm targeting Director of Engineering and VP of Engineering roles where team development and delivery excellence are priorities.

roles.title: Director of Engineering

skills.canonical_name: translate ambiguous business requirements into clear technical strategies

voice_samples.text: Change management is about controlling how changes reach your production systems. Rather than letting engineers push updates whenever they want, you put a process in place: someone other than the author reviews the change, it gets tested somewhere safe before it touches live systems, and there’s a written record of what happened. An auditor reviewing this will pull a sample of changes from the audit period and check that each one followed the process. If a developer can quietly ship code to production without anyone signing off, that’s the kind of gap the audit is designed to catch.

Least privilege follows similar logic but applies to who can get into what. The idea is that people should only have the access their role requires, and nothing more. A support agent doesn’t need the keys to the production database, and a former employee shouldn’t be able to log in at all. In practice this means avoiding shared accounts, removing access promptly when someone changes roles or leaves, and reviewing permissions on a regular schedule so they don’t quietly accumulate over time.

These two areas tend to be where companies lose the most points, because both depend on discipline rather than technology. You can buy a tool that manages access, but the audit cares whether you actually revoked the right person’s login last quarter and whether someone genuinely reviewed that risky deployment. The controls are simple to describe and easy to let slip, which is exactly why auditors spend so much time on them.

Engineering leadership lives or dies in the unglamorous moments. Anyone can look competent when a launch goes well and the dashboards are green, but the real test is how you show up on the bad days: the outage that drags past midnight, the project that quietly slipped two months, the talented engineer who’s clearly one frustrating sprint away from updating their resume. Morale isn’t built through pizza parties or motivational Slack messages. It’s built when people see that their manager takes the blame upward and passes the credit downward, protects focus time instead of stacking on meetings, and is honest when something isn’t working rather than papering over it with optimism. Teams can tell the difference between a leader who actually has their back and one who’s performing the role, and they’ll give their best work to the former every time.
